stem: Which description best characterizes T1 relaxation?

options.text: Recovery of longitudinal magnetization through energy exchange with the surrounding lattice
Decay of transverse magnetization from spin-spin dephasing
Loss of signal caused by spins leaving the selected slice
Reversal of gradient-induced phase during readout

explanation: T1, or spin-lattice relaxation, restores longitudinal magnetization as excited spins transfer energy to their environment. T2 is the distinct process of transverse phase decoherence.

stem: At one T1 time after a 90-degree excitation, approximately how much equilibrium longitudinal magnetization has recovered?
explanation: T1 is defined as the time required for longitudinal magnetization to recover to about 63% of equilibrium. The 37% value is the fraction of transverse magnetization remaining after one T2.

stem: Why is fat usually bright relative to simple fluid on a T1-weighted image?

options.text: Fat has a longer T1 and remains saturated for less time
Fat has no transverse relaxation after excitation
Fat has a shorter T1 and recovers longitudinal magnetization more rapidly
Water cannot absorb RF energy at the hydrogen resonance frequency

explanation: Fat's molecular environment permits relatively efficient spin-lattice energy exchange, giving it a short T1. It is therefore ready to contribute more signal when the next excitation occurs after a short TR.

stem: How does increasing the main magnetic field generally affect tissue T1 values?

options.text: It shortens all T1 values to zero
It leaves T1 unchanged while eliminating T2 decay
It makes T1 equal to T2 in every tissue

explanation: T1 values generally increase at higher field strengths because energy exchange at the higher Larmor frequency is less efficient for many tissues. The magnitude varies by tissue, but T1 does not become identical to T2.

stem: Which statement correctly relates T1 and T2 relaxation after excitation?

options.text: They occur simultaneously but describe different physical processes
T1 must finish before T2 can begin
T2 restores longitudinal magnetization after T1 removes it
They are two names for the same exponential decay

explanation: Longitudinal recovery and transverse decay begin together after excitation, but they reflect different interactions. In biological tissues, T1 is usually longer than T2.
